coredata.dc:title: Challenges and progresses in the chemical investigation of high molecular weight species in condensed pyrolysis products of coal and biomass

coredata.dc:description: The condensed products of pyrolysis embrace a wide range of compounds, ranging from relatively low molecular weight (MW) hydrocarbons ("light tar") to progressively heavier species, including large PAHs ("heavy tar"), ending up with fine carbonaceous particles (soot) of nanometric and, in some cases, even micrometric size. While most part of "light tar” from either coal or biomass pyrolysis can be effectively detected by conventional gas chromatography/mass spectrometry (GC/MS), a detailed analysis of “heavy tar” and soot turns out to be rather cumbersome and more susceptible of errors, especially when the techniques, originally developed for fossil fuels-derived products, are applied to the oxygen rich biomass products. Size exclusion chromatography (SEC) coupled with UV-Visible (UV-Vis) absorption detector is applied to both coal and biomass “heavy tar”, providing evidence of high mass components reaching MWs of thousands of u, similar to incipient soot. Carbon particles reach the micrometric scale in some coal pyrolysis products, so that "ad hoc" solvent-based separation procedures have been developed in order to distinguish soot from char fines. In biomass pyrolysis products, carbon rich particulate remains confined to the nano-size range. The latter exhibits fluorescence emission in the green wavelength range, with a relatively high quantum efficiency, and can therefore be regarded as carbon dots. Recent progress shows that the molecular mass and size of the heaviest species in biomass heavy pyrolysis products can be assessed by SEC with a fluorescence detector. However, the technique still requires refinement due to the lack of SEC calibration data valid for all tar species. Laser desorption ionization time of flight mass spectrometry (LDI TOFMS) turns out to be a valid support to evaluate the molecular range of such ill-defined products.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The condensed products of pyrolysis embrace a wide range of compounds, ranging from relatively low molecular weight (MW) hydrocarbons ("light tar") to progressively heavier species, including large PAHs ("heavy tar"), ending up with fine carbonaceous particles (soot) of nanometric and, in some cases, even micrometric size. While most part of "light tar” from either coal or biomass pyrolysis can be effectively detected by conventional gas chromatography/mass spectrometry (GC/MS), a detailed analysis of “heavy tar” and soot turns out to be rather cumbersome and more susceptible of errors, especially when the techniques, originally developed for fossil fuels-derived products, are applied to the oxygen rich biomass products. Size exclusion chromatography (SEC) coupled with UV-Visible (UV-Vis) absorption detector is applied to both coal and biomass “heavy tar”, providing evidence of high mass components reaching MWs of thousands of u, similar to incipient soot. Carbon particles reach the micrometric scale in some coal pyrolysis products, so that "ad hoc" solvent-based separation procedures have been developed in order to distinguish soot from char fines. In biomass pyrolysis products, carbon rich particulate remains confined to the nano-size range. The latter exhibits fluorescence emission in the green wavelength range, with a relatively high quantum efficiency, and can therefore be regarded as carbon dots. Recent progress shows that the molecular mass and size of the heaviest species in biomass heavy pyrolysis products can be assessed by SEC with a fluorescence detector. However, the technique still requires refinement due to the lack of SEC calibration data valid for all tar species. Laser desorption ionization time of flight mass spectrometry (LDI TOFMS) turns out to be a valid support to evaluate the molecular range of such ill-defined products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pyrolysis of solid fuels, including coal and biomass, generates, besides the residual solid, called char, a large array of volatile compounds, which at room temperature remain gaseous or else condense forming a liquid product. The condensable fraction of the pyrolysis products has been the object of extensive research [1–8] and has been referred to in the literature with different terms, namely pyrolysis liquids, tar, oil, bio-oil, bio-crude. The label of tar is usually used for the condensable products of pyrolysis of whichever feedstock (coal, biomass, wastes etc), when these are not desired and not specifically targeted, whereas the terms oil and bio-oil or bio-crude are applied to the liquid products of, respectively, coal and biomass pyrolysis, when there is an interest for their commercial use [9] as liquid fuels or as chemical raw materials [10].

The yield, chemical composition and physical properties of the condensable products of coal and biomass pyrolysis, as a matter of fact, are very variable, depending on one side on the feedstock’s characteristics and pretreatment, on the other side on the operating conditions of the pyrolysis process, which can be roughly grouped in three categories: 1. slow or mild pyrolysis conditions, where temperatures are limited to generally less than 600 °C, heating rates do not exceed the order of magnitude of 10 K/min and holding/residence times are in the order of tens of minutes, 2. fast pyrolysis conditions, with heating rates of 100–1000 °C/min, temperatures above 800 °C and residence times in the order of seconds or minutes, 3. very fast or flash pyrolysis conditions, with even heating rates, of 10000 °C/min and above, and holding times of seconds and fractions of seconds.

Chemical analysis of the condensed pyrolysis products is very challenging because the chemistry of pyrolysis is complex, involving not only the rupture of the macromolecular structure of coals and biomass, but also the successive evolution of the (primary) pyrolysis products.

Fletcher et al. [11–14] developed the CPD [15–17] model to predict the rupture of coals macromolecular structure, the formation and successive evolution of tar and gas. They used nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy to characterize the complex structure of coal, tar and char, in terms of average aromatic cluster size, number of bridges and loops per cluster. However, the structure of coal tars was found to be significantly different from that of the parent coal, in particular the number of condensed aromatic carbons and the MW per cluster are significantly lower [11–14]. The primary products from coal pyrolysis, in fact, include both small and large condensed molecules with aromatic and aliphatic portions [11–14] which, if temperature is high enough, can undergo further reactions in the gas phase with generation of heavier aromatics as polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and eventually even soot [18,19]. In this framework, the “heavy tar” compounds can be considered precursors of soot [20] so much so that sometimes the borderline between large PAHs and soot is hard to draw [21].

The macromolecular structure of biomass is also complex because of the coexistence of different bio-polymers (cellulose, hemicellulose and lignin). Wire mesh experiments on the single biomass components carried out by Hoekstra et al. [22] indicated, indeed, levoglucosan (C6H10O5, 162 gmol-1) as the main primary volatile product of cellulose pyrolysis. Similarly, Gong et al. [23] obtained levoglucosan, cellobiosan (C12H20O10, 324 gmol-1) and cellotriosan (C18H32O16, 504 gmol-1) from cellulose. Senneca et al. [24] reported as primary pyrolysis products mainly levoglucosan for hemicellulose and cellulose, vanillin (C8H8O3, 152 gmol-1) and heavier compounds for lignin. The primary products from cellulose and hemicellulose pyrolysis undergo cracking reactions and produce permanent gases, while the primary compounds produced by lignin evolve into secondary tar, rich in phenols. Eventually, bio-oils contain hundreds of organic components (carboxylic acids, phenols, alcohols, aldehydes, ketones, ethers, esters, furans, anhydrosugars, and water) [25]. They include only a minor fraction of aliphatics and much larger shares of oxygenated compounds compared to coal’s pyrolysis products. Altogether, the tendency to soot formation is lower for biomass compared with coal.

Several papers focused on the analysis of the oils produced by slow and fast pyrolysis of coal and biomass [26–28]. The methods for the characterization of these products, in particular bio-oils, aim in particular to check their potential use as advanced biofuels or as a source of valuable chemicals. In particular, the methods include measurements of acidity, viscosity, boiling points, etc. Methods for a thorough characterization of the MW of bio-oils are reported in literature beings these products derived in large amounts from purposely developed pyrolytic processes optimizing their yields and massive production. Accurate analysis of very high molecular mass compounds and incipient soot has not been considered a major issue for these applications due to the low temperatures of the processes [29].

The condensable products of pyrolysis at highest heating rates and temperatures (> 10000 °C/min, >1300 °C) have been investigated over the last decades in a different context, in particular in the frame of combustion and gasification of coal [30–40], and more recently of biomass [22–24,41–44]. In industrial pulverized boilers or gasifiers, tar formation can cause operational problems, such as corrosion and blockage of exhaust lines, moreover generation and combustion of volatiles are important for the temperature profiles that are accomplished inside the reactors. Notably at the high temperatures typical of these reactors, larger fractions of heavy- high MW compounds are produced [27,45,46] and could evolve into soot [18,19,47–52], ultimately impacting also on radiative heat transfer in the volatile flame region. Incipient particle sampling and analytical methods suitable for these high MW compounds are worth of investigation in this type of reactors, but has received relatively little attention so far in the literature [18,19,49,53–55].

From the picture drawn, it can be concluded that there is a need for experimental and analytical methods suitable to detect high MW species and soot in the condensable products of pyrolysis and that this issue is particularly relevant in very fast pyrolysis conditions with the aim to control and study thermoconversion phenomena. The shift in the research focus from coal to biomass has added complexity to this objective. This is because oxygenated compounds present in biomass can affect the reliability of established diagnostic techniques used for MW estimation [56].

The scope of the present work is to provide an overview on the most used methods for MW metrics of the condensable fraction of coal and biomass pyrolysis products. The work is not meant to comprehensively cover all the work done on the characterization of pyrolysis oils/tars, but rather to:
1.
summarize the most consolidated methods used in the laboratories worldwide
2.
highlight the limits of the characterization of high MW species and soot in the products of very fast pyrolysis of coal and biomass, based on the experience done at STEMS-CNR.
3.
describe the possible approaches for overcoming these limits.
Research needs and preliminary results obtained with new methods are also presented in the final part of the paper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methods used in the laboratories worldwide for MW determination in pyrolysis liquids
ce:section.ce:para: The analysis of complex mixtures such as the condensable products of coal and biomass is not an easy task. All the experimental approaches and methods proposed to analyze the MW and composition of condensable pyrolysis products have some strengths and also some drawbacks and limitations. For this reason, multiple methods are applied very often for the analysis of a given sample, providing complementary information. Table 1 summarizes the experimental approaches most commonly adopted in recent works. In Table 1 it is also highlighted the ranges of MW investigated: They are very variable, according with the MW range of investigation of the employed technique ( Table 1).

The two most used techniques are gas chromatography (GC) and Gel Permeation Chromatography (GPC), often coupled with a preliminary solvent extraction to simplify the chemical complexity of the fractions to analyse [45,46]. These techniques will be briefly described in the following.

For many years, the chemical composition of tars and oils has been mainly analyzed using GC, particularly when coupled with a mass spectrometric detector (GC-MS) or flame ionization detector (GC-FID). GC-MS method yielded important insights into the molecular structures of the substances found in liquids derived from pyrolysis [28]. However, it presents several drawbacks as:
•
the necessity to evaluate the response factor of each species or classes of species for its quantification through the use of analytical standards,
•
the necessity to compare the mass spectra of tar components with as wide as possible MS library,
•
the impossibility to analyse non volatile species.

Despite the mentioned limitations, GC-MS is still the most used technique for a preliminary examination of liquid pyrolysis products. Manuel Garcia-Perez et al. [58] were able to identify by GC-MS up to 34 mass % of the compounds of tars derived from Mallee Woody Biomass as a function of temperature between 350 and 580 °C, and obtained complementary information on the heavy components from UV-Visible fluorescence spectroscopy and thermogravimetry. To overcome the problem of peaks coelution, Themistoklis Sfetsas et al. [61] performed two-dimensional gas chromatography with time-of-flight mass spectrometry (GC × GC–TOFMS), and were able to identify more than 70 % of total chromatogram peaks.

However, the need to study also the non volatile part of the samples, requires the development of other methods, such as GPC. In this liquid chromatography method, the column is filled with a porous gel material. Analytes are separated according to their hydrodynamic volume, indeed large species elute faster than small ones, because the small analytes have to diffuse through a wide network of small pores, which is, instead, bypassed by the larger analytes. Generally, refractive index or UV-Visible detectors are used to detect analytes. GPC requires very small amount of samples, however it does not allow a fine separation of the analytes, so that relatively broad MW fractions are segregated and identified. To overcome this problem, rather than using just one column to investigate the entire range of MWs of interest [75], it is possible to repeat the analysis using multiple different columns, each suitable for the analysis of a narrower sub-range of MWs.

A further element of concern that has to be taken into account in the analysis of pyrolysis condensates is the validity of standards, against the coexistence in the samples of a multitude of species characterized not only by largely different MWs, but also by different functionalities [76].

An array of advanced techniques has been proposed for characterizing high-mass, complex and polydispersed hydrocarbon mixtures by a research group from the Imperial College [32,77]. Thanks to the experience acquired on the compounds with aromatic moieties and long alkyl chains as asphalthenes, Imperial College scientists further on used Size Exclusion Chromatography (SEC), to analyse both poorly and highly alkylated coal tar [32], using as eluents, respectively 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidinone (NMP) and a NMP-chloroform mixture. Indeed, the aliphaticity and other kinds of functionalities may have an effect on the MW evaluated by SEC [78]. Coal tar in both solvents was also analysed by synchronous fluorescence, able to discriminate among classes of different sized aromatic chromophores. Moreover, the Imperial College scientists performed laser desorption mass spectrometry of tar and of tar fractions separated by thin layer chromatography (TLC). Mass spectrometry of the relatively immobile material after TLC fractionation allowed identifying small quantities of large species, whereas the mass spectra of the more mobile fractions resembled the spectrum of the unseparated sample. The use of a wide array of diagnostic techniques was shown to be a successful approach to analyse complex chemical mixtures such as coal tar.

In another paper [79], the Imperial College’s research group applied a novel multitechnique approach to tar from co-gasification of biomass and coal, in order to study their aging, and they disclosed some relevant differences with respect to tar produced from coal alone. No applications to tar from biomass pyrolysis, alone, have been reported by the mentioned group.

From this brief overview on existing literature it is clear that much has been done on the light components of tars/oils with GC-MS analysis and GPC systems. However, the heavier fractions, insoluble in solvent as dichloromethane (DCM), acetone and Tetrahydrofuran (THF), is often ignored, even if it could represent a not negligible part of the samples. The survey also suggests that a detailed chemico-physical characterization of complex mixtures composed by polydisperse MW distribution, from small molecules to large aggregates, needs a comprehensive diagnostic strategy, including proper sampling, separation and analytical methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of products of very fast biomass and coal pyrolysis at STEMS
ce:section.ce:para: Two different reactors, i.e., a drop tube reactor (DTR) and in a heated strip reactor (HSR), have been used obtaining similar particles temperature and heating rates, while the volatiles experienced quite different time-temperature histories and were collected by means of different sampling strategies, which lead to differences in tar composition. The volatiles produced in the DTR were entrained in a hot gas stream and captured at the reactor outlet by tar traps. In the HSR, the volatiles released from the pyrolysing coal or biomass particles encountered a cold atmosphere, which minimized the secondary reactions of volatile products and condensed on an overarching cold glass bridge. Details on the pyrolysis reactors and experiments can be found in references [24,40–44,47,80–83]. Our previous studies were focused on fast heating rate reactors (≈103 - 104 K/min) operating at high temperature (1273–2073 K), in both inert (N2) or reactive atmosphere (CO2) in order to investigate the pyrolysis products obtained in the same conditions used for PF combustors and entrained flow gasifiers. More details on the raw fuels used are reported in [24,40–44,47,80–83].

The pyrolysis products included tar with large distribution of MW and in some cases, also very fine particles, assimilable with incipient soot. In order to analyse such polydisperse mixture of products, the Naples’ group adopted a methodology which included, as first and fundamental step, the separation of pyrolytic products of very different MWs in three sub-fractions, named Light tar, Heavy tar and Soot. The method used for separation and analysis of the three sub-fraction are shortly summarized in the following, highlighting the criticalities emerged during the experimental campaign and, finally the lessons learnt.

The tar samples have been recovered by washing the glassware (the glass tubes in the case of the DTR and the pyrex bridge in the case of HSR) by solvents. The selection of the solvent is not trivial and can be different for coal and biomass tar. Different solvents have indeed been tested in our lab as well as in previous literature. Tetrahydrofuran (THF), used for instance by [84], was very effective in dissolving coal tar. However, THF can polymerize at ambient temperature, contaminating and complicating the samples analysis [85].

In ref [14] deuterated DCM was used to dissolve the tar, which was found to be only partly soluble in that solvent. The soluble tar fraction was examined using 13C NMR method designed for liquid phases, whereas the remaining insoluble tar fraction was examined by solid-state NMR, similarly to coal and char. We also used DCM that was found to partially dissolve the tar from biomass. Indeed, it is quite good at dissolving most aliphatic and unsubstituted aromatic species present in tar, but it is not likewise good with oxygenated aromatic compounds, besides, it has the disadvantage of being cancerogenic. The solvent most commonly and satisfactorily used in the authors’ works is instead acetone, which has a low toxicity and a higher solubility power toward the oxygenated species, along with a comparable solubility for the aromatic/aliphatic compounds with respect to DCM. The condensate tar material dissolved in acetone has been called “light tar”. In most cases, acetone turned to be unable to dissolve all the tarry material recovered from the glass tubes and/or the pyrex bridge of the DTR and HSR. The acetone-insoluble fraction was dissolved in the more powerful solvent NMP and the obtained sample has been named “heavy tar”. The presence of a fraction insoluble in DCM and constituted by high MW compounds was already highlighted [27] and often called GC-undetectable species [45,46]. In this regard we can anticipate that a large proportion of the soluble species in acetone was found to be undetectable by GC-MS as will be described in detail in the following. However, it has to be underlined that the use of NMP, so far just employed as GPC eluent, was introduced by our group and allowed to recover and dissolved all the high MW compounds formed during pyrolytic processes.

This heaviest fraction dissolved in NMP, in some cases, can include also very large molecules and nanoparticles, which can be regarded as nascent soot. However, in DTR coal pyrolysis experiments [40], especially with CO2 rich atmosphere, soot-like material was collected together with the char particles by means of the solid sampling equipment: a ceramic filter positioned after the oil-cooled probe [86]. This evidence requested the development of a procedure for the separation of soot from other products and to discriminate it from the fine char particles, which could possibly be generated through fragmentation. A procedure previously proposed by Veranth et al. [49], has been followed and optimized by Apicella et al. as described in [87]. The procedure is based on carbon particles suspension in ethyl alcohol, settling, and decanting, and repeating the procedure until a stable top ethyl alcohol suspension is formed. Soot is separated with the top fraction while char remains in the bottom fraction.
For the reader’s convenience, Fig. 1 reports a scheme of the samples investigated.

As first approach, the “light tar” products of coal pyrolysis in HSR and DTR were analysed with GC-MS technique [47]. The species identified from the chromatograms have been grouped in classes of interest for either coal or biomass pyrolysis products:
For coal: light and heavy aliphatics (C12-C20 and C21-C30), oxygen-containing-aromatic compounds, PAHs with ring numbers from 2 up to > 5 rings.

For biomass: aliphatics (alkanes and alkenes), aromatic oxygenated compounds (aldehydes and alcohols), aromatics and PAHs with C ≤ 16 (Light PAHs), PAHs with C > 16 (Heavy PAHs), monomers/anhydrosugars.
In order to calculate the concentration values of individual species, standard samples of known concentrations where used:
For coal: aliphatics with C ≤ 18, PAHs mixture with MW up to 300 u supplied by Supelco EPA 525 PAH mix) [41].
For biomass: phenol, levoglucosan and vanillin samples from Sigma Aldrich [41].

In regard the species detectable by GC-MS, it was noticed that light and heavy aliphatics along with PAHs were the main components of DTR tar, whereas heavy aliphatics and oxy compounds were predominant in the HSR tar. Moreover, no soot was observed in the HSR system. These findings confirmed that the occurrence of secondary reactions of tar was avoided in the HSR thanks to the immediate cooling down of volatiles ejected in the cold environment.
Thus, the HSR system was selected as an appropriate reactor for primary tar investigation [24,41–43,47].

Fig. 2 A-C show exemplary chromatograms of “light tar”, obtained from pyrolysis in the HSR of coal, of a selected raw biomass, and of cellulose as individual biomass components. All samples were produced in HSR at 1273–1573 K in N2.

In the case of coal (Fig. 2 A), saturated and unsaturated aliphatic species from dodecane to triacontane are the main components and constitute the aliphatic class. Indeed, the chromatogram in Fig. 2A resembles a typical chromatogram of hydrocarbon samples. Phenols and similar oxygenated aromatics are less abundant and constitute the oxy class. Eventually, PAHs from 2 to > 5 ring composed the PAH class [41].
The “light tar” of natural biomass appears much more complex (Fig. 2B) than coal.

Notably, in the case of walnut shells, a biomass with high lignin content, oxo-aromatics were mainly constituted of methoxy-aromatic compounds related to lignin structure (syringaldehyde, vanillin, eugenol and coniferyl alcohol).

The chromatogram of “light tar” of cellulose (Fig. 2 C) is similar to that obtained from hemicellulose (not reported) showing a very remarkable peak of levoglucosane (over 95 %) for cellulose and anhydro-xylopyranose for hemicellulose, resulting from the fragmentation of the constitutive polysaccharides [24,41].

As a first approach, the “heavy tar” products were analysed with SEC technique washing the pyrex bridge of the HSR with NMP [24,41,42]. Fig. 3 A-F shows exemplary chromatograms and UV-Vis absorption spectra of the “heavy tar” obtained in the HSR from the pyrolysis of coal, of a selected raw biomass, and of cellulose as representative of an individual biomass component.

The SEC analysis of “heavy tar” reports the intensity of the signal versus the retention time (Fig. 3 A, C, E). The main difference observed between the SEC of coal and biomass lies in the presence of a peak at retention times of approximately 6 min. This early peak, observed in raw biomass, was also found in the tar obtained from cellulose and hemicellulose; it was not observed instead in the tar produced from lignin pyrolysis [24,41]. From the retention time, it is possible to calculate the MW and size of the chemical species. It must be, however, recalled that this calculation may be affected by two criticalities:
1.
The calculation of MW distributions from retention time relies on calibration standards;
2.
The calculation of molecules’ size from MW relies on a reasonable assumption on the sample density [41], which is, again, valid for fossil fuel derived tar.

If the standard calibration curves of coal and other fossil fuel derived products are applied (such as using as standard compounds polystyrenes and carbonaceous samples [88–90]), the early peak at 6 min corresponds to a size of 100 nm. However, this result could not be correct if oxygenated compounds, typical of biomass products, are largely present.

In the UV-Vis absorption spectra of “heavy tar” reported in Fig. 3 B, D, F the signals obtained appear broad and structureless, which is interpreted as due to the presence of aromatic moieties with a bigger extension (above 7 rings) with respect to GC−MS PAHs [41].

The results confirm that the borderline between very large molecules detected by SEC and soot nanoparticles is labile, indeed soot can indeed be formed by secondary reactions within the volatile species, provided that the temperature and the residence times are sufficient.

In the coal DTR pyrolysis experiments, in particular those carried out in CO2 atmosphere, a distinguishable fraction of very fine carbon particulate (0.1–1 µm) was recovered by the solids sampling probe [40]. It was therefore necessary to understand the origin and the nature of this particulate and in particular, if this was the result of a soot formation process or rather of char fragmentation phenomena.

After the application of the separation procedure described in [86,87], carbon particulate was analysed by TGA, Raman, FTIR, XPS, SEM and Laser granulometry. The same analytical techniques were applied to the larger char particles (10–100 µm). The results are summarized in Table 2. In particular, the soot characteristics have been compared with those of char.

It may be remarked that the absence of inorganic components in the soot further confirms that it was not originated from fragmentation of the parent coal or char, but it was rather formed in the volatile phase, from the high temperature evolution of volatile components [91]. In spite of the different formation route of soot and char fines, the two fractions had similar size of aromatic layers, yet frankly different combustion reactivity, maybe arising from the catalytic effect of inorganic matter in char, which is totally absent in soot.

In biomass DTR pyrolysis experiments, the sampling probe of the reactor did not collect, mixed with char, an amount of soot enough high to be separated from char with the procedure previously described. However, the SEC of “heavy tar” obtained in the HSR (Fig. 3C) revealed the presence of large carbon rich compounds, even reaching the nano-scale, which can be considered incipient soot particles.
Pros and contra of the main analytical approaches used to investigate condensable products of biomass and coal pyrolysis are now open to discussion.

GC-MS analysis has to be considered in primis, being the most commonly used technique for the analysis of tars and oils in previous literature [92–96]. Based on MS libraries and standard species analysis, GC-MS is able to identify single compounds and to determine their structures up to a certain molecular mass and boiling point, along with their concentration. But, before entering the chromatographic column, samples have to be vaporized and usually the compounds with a mass higher than 300 u and a boiling point higher than 573 K cannot be analysed. Moreover, the species with boiling points lower than that of the employed solvent usually are overlaid by the solvent peak in the chromatogram. Summing up, GC-MS is able to analyse just a part of the pyrolysis liquid products [33]. These problems become particularly relevant in the case of the tar obtained by very fast pyrolysis of biomass, for which it was found that:
1.
Not all the tar dissolves in acetone (only a fraction does, i.e., the “light tar”);
2.
Only a fraction of the species in the “light tar” is detectable by GC-MS (only the fraction constituted of molecules <300 u does);
3.
There is a not negligible portion of acetone-insoluble but NMP-soluble tar, i.e., the “heavy tar”. The wt% of the “heavy tar” has been estimated to be less than 30 % for both coal and biomass tar samples.
Thus, further techniques are mandatory, in order to close the mass balance on pyrolysis products.

Very few works have been devoted to analyse these samples as a whole, taking into account also the compounds not detectable with the GC-MS. Solid-state13C NMR of the coal tar was used to investigate its chemical structure [11–15]. In the cited papers, the authors derived values of the number of carbon atoms per cluster ranging from 10 to 21, corresponding to average MW values from 164 to 410 u. Some other techniques, like H‐NMR, FTIR, UV-Vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy have been already proposed at Imperial College for the coal tar analysis [32]. Even though unable to provide a quantitative information on tar composition, these techniques provided details on the functional groups, aromaticity and size of the tar compounds and on their aliphatic and aromatic hydrogen content. SEC has also been used to provide information on the MW distributions of the tar produced by coal pyrolysis [15,38].

The most critical aspect of performing SEC measurements emerging in the authors’ experience is the definition of a calibration curve that allows transforming elution times in MW and that is valid for all the species composing biomass tar [76,96,97]. The problem arises from the heterogeneous composition of the samples and the choice of standards with chemical composition and conformational geometries similar to those featuring tar compounds to construct a reliable calibration curve. This is especially true for biomass tar samples composed by a plethora of oxygen rich compounds, which are different from coal tar ones, not only in terms of MW, but also of chemical functionalities.

Few groups in the literature have tried to use standards [15,37,39,41] to assess MW/size from SEC of coal derived tar. Among these, Freihaut et al. [37] who calculated the average MW of tar using different standards and correlated them with coal rank. A similar work is needed also for biomass tar. The Imperial College group already found that when molecules have oxygenated functionalities, SEC is not nearly as independent from chemical structure as it is for aromatic compounds, even with aliphatic functionalities, and for various types of polymers [56]. By using highly oxygenated polysaccharide samples with different MW, they traced a different curve with respect to the generally used polystyrene (PS) calibration curve.

New methodologies are under investigation to overcome the criticalities highlighted in the previous sections, and better characterize the heavy fraction of pyrolysis products which cannot be easily analysed by GC-MS (either because it is not soluble in the solvents used for GC-MS or because it is not detectable by GC-MS).

Two approaches are being followed in order to improve SEC analysis of the critical “heavy tar”:
1.
The use of a different SEC column (a highly cross-linked “individual-pore” column), able to separate with a higher resolution the species in the molecular and nanometric range (100–20000 u).
2.
The use of a second detector based on fluorescence spectroscopy, in order to acquire also fluorescence spectra on the maximum of each peak for improving the peak attribution to oxygenated or not oxygenated compounds.

New methodologies have been tested on some of the most interesting and critical samples that had been screened in previous campaigns. Pyrolysis conditions, which favoured the production of heavier compounds, have been chosen. Moreover, the focus has been put on biomass, because their analysis is more problematic then coal’s. Accordingly, new methodologies have been applied firstly to a tar derived from very fast pyrolysis of a model biomass compound, namely hemicellulose and, secondly, to tar derived from a natural biomass under the same conditions. Both the “light tar” and “heavy tar” tar produced from pyrolysis of hemicellulose (xylan) in the HSR at 2073 K exhibited features typical of a new intriguing class of materials, the carbon dots.

Along this path, a first interesting result has been obtained for hemicellulose (xylan) tar produced in the HSR at 2073 K. In this case, “light tar” and “heavy tar” exhibited features typical of a new intriguing class of materials, the carbon dots (CDs), which have attracted broad research interest for their interesting fluorescent properties [98]. Indeed, “light” and “heavy” tar, coupled to nanometric dimensions, presented a fluorescence emission in the blue and green range, respectively, as shown in Fig. 4 [80].

The fluorescence spectra of “heavy tar” ( Fig. 5) exhibited a broader signal with a tail extended at higher wavelength values with respect to the “light tar”, due to the larger extension of aromatic moieties [80].

SEC analysis of these samples was carried out with two detectors: HP1050 UV–Vis diode array detector that measures the absorbance signal at a fixed absorption wavelength (350 nm) and a G1321AR emission detector. More details are reported in [75].
Absorption and fluorescence chromatograms for hemicellulose (xylan) “heavy tar” are reported in Fig. 6.

This analysis easily distinguishes and individuates the molecular fluorophores often widely spread in synthesized CDs allowing to obtain relevant optical parameters as optical band gap and quantum yield, detailed throughout the size distribution, more or less broad, featuring CDs.

The bulk quantum yield (QY) at 350 nm of the sample was found to be 7.7 %. With the method reported in [75], it is possible to derive the QY values as a function of the elution time and they are reported in Fig. 6 on the apexes of the main peaks. As the elution time increases, i.e., the MW decreases, the QY reaches increasingly high values. Optimized solvent-extraction is ongoing to separate only the more fluorescent fraction, i.e. species eluting after 13 min, to improve the sample bulk QY.

However, their QY is already comparable with that of other CDs and it represents a very interesting result in view of the large potential of green CDs in electronics and sensoristic applications [98–102].

SEC coupled with UV-Vis absorption and fluorescence spectrometers as detectors has been, in a second instance, applied to “heavy tar” and “light tar” produced in DTR from walnut shells. Fig. 7A-B show the results obtained by “individual-pore” column with both absorption and fluorescence detector for tars produced by pyrolysis of walnut shells in DTR.

It is possible to observe from the Fig. 7 A and B, that both “light tar” and “heavy tar” present a multimodal dispersion, ranging from molecular up to particle-size region. Species with a MW value higher than 105 u (roughly corresponding to a particle diameter of about 5 nm) elute as a unique peak above the exclusion limit of the column. These species have been investigated in details on a wide-MW range column (Fig. 3 A, C, E). Not all the peaks presented both absorption and fluorescence signals. Earlier eluting peaks, which are the heavier ones, are only visible with the absorption detection and they can be identified as very large aromatic species with nanometric size.

In agreement with the Imperial College group [56], it is found that the use of PS calibration curve overestimates the MW of oxygenated compounds, as demonstrated by the analysis of a standard sample of vanillin (C8H8O3, MW: 152 u). Indeed, two different standards (Vanilline MW= 152 u and Pullulan Polymer MW= 342), whose SEC chromatograms are reported in Fig. 7B, present a lower elution time in comparison to polystyrene standards used for the calibration curve reported in Fig. 8. From the analysis of the elution of the oxygenated and not oxygenated standards it was found that all the species with a MW ≤ 300 u, which represents the threshold value for GC-MS detection, elute from 14 min up 19 min. Based on this observation, it turns out that a consistent fraction of the “light tar” dissolved in acetone (roughly the 40 % on the basis of the molecular region of the SEC chromatogram area) is not analysable by GC-MS and corresponds to the SEC chromatogram between 8 and 14 min

The biomass “light tar” obtained in DTR (Fig. 7B) has been analysed also with LDI TOFMS and the obtained spectrum is reported in Fig. 9. “Light tar” samples have been analysed by Laser desorption ionization time of flight mass spectrometry (LDI TOFMS). LDI TOFMS spectra were recorded on positive reflectron mode on a SCIEX TOF/TOF™ 5800 System using a N2 laser (λ = 337 nm). The target was prepared by depositing on a standard stainless steel plate the sample dissolved in acetone, which is left to evaporate at room temperature.
The obtained spectra were quantitatively analyzed using Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) analysis and graphing software (Origin). More details on the method are reported in [104].

It is possible to observe that the MW extends up to 900–1000 u, which means much above the GC-MS limit (300 u). Integrating over the entire MW distribution reported in Fig. 9, it turns out that the “light tar” with m/z ≤ 300 (i.e., the fraction detectable by GC-MS) counts for 32 %. This result confirms that roughly the 60–70 % of the “light tar” dissolved in acetone is not analysable by GC-MS.

In a previous paper [104] it is reported that the application to very complex mass spectra of a mathematical method, the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) analysis, allows an easier interpretation of them. Indeed, the method provides the periodic mass differences of peak sequences, which can be interpreted in terms of the presence of specific chemical functionalities. In particular, recent investigations [105] concerning the application of FFT mathematical analysis to LDI TOFMS of complex carbonaceous species [104], reported that the presence of m/z 16 gaps indicates the presence of oxygen functionalities [105].
In the present paper, the FFT analysis was applied on the spectrum of the biomass “light tar” obtained in DTR (Fig. 9) and the obtained height-normalized FFT profile was reported in Fig. 10.

The FFT profile (Fig. 10) presents a peak at m/z 12.6 suggesting the occurrence of homologous series increasing by a carbon atom or CH and CH2 group (gap of m/z 12–14, with a prevalence of 12) [104] and a peak at m/z 16, which indicates the presence of oxygenated functionalities in the growing species, also in a MW above the detection limit of the GC-MS.

The condensed products of coal and biomass pyrolysis include a wide range of compounds, ranging from the molecular size to fine carbonaceous particles of nano and in some cases even micrometric size (soot).
Figs. 11–12 summarize the efforts of the authors in regard to the development of analytical methods for the chemical and the structural characterization as well as the mass identification of the less studied and most ill-defined pyrolysis products. The “light” part of the tar from either coal or biomass pyrolysis with molecular size up to 300 u and boiling point up to 573 K can be effectively detected by GC-MS. Instead, information on the heavier components (>300 u) can be obtained by means of a multi-technique approach, combined with optimized solvent-separation procedure, as schematically reported in Figs. 11–12 for coal and biomass, respectively. SEC coupled with absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy has provided evidence and MW distributions of these high MW components featuring the “light” and the “heavy” tar. However, careful has to be taken to retrieve MW from SEC analysis when oxygenated compounds are largely present as for biomass-derived samples. More reliable information on the mass and the chemical functionalities, even if in a smaller mass range, can be obtained by LDI TOFMS.

Carbonaceous particulates have been found to be often composed not only by char but also by submicron soot-like particles. Purposely developed solvent-based separation method has been set up to allow a detailed characterization of the soot fraction.

The structural characterization and mass identification of the less studied and ill-defined pyrolysis products, the components of tar > 300 u and soot, are fundamental for a correct model prediction and for the mass balance closure of biomass thermochemical conversion processes. These compounds represent also a valuable source of high performance carbon materials as carbon dots as well as a valuable feedstock for obtaining value-added chemicals and for the synthesis of liquid fuels.
